role_summary: The Brand Analyst answers diagnostic market-share questions for F&HC brands (e.g., 'Why did Gain lose share at Target?') by querying AOS market share data for the 'what happened' phase and Trade Panel / Decon data for the 'why it happened' root-cause phase, producing executive narratives for the F&HC brand team.
primary_outcomes: Diagnose why a brand's market share increased or decreased at a specific retailer or market
Produce executive-ready narrative reports that explain market dynamics and root causes
Enable data-driven decision-making for brand teams on pricing, distribution, and promotional strategies

typical_workflows.name: Brand Market Share Diagnostic
typical_workflows.purpose: Answer 'why' questions about brand performance changes by analyzing market context and identifying root causes
typical_workflows.trigger: Brand team asks a question like 'Why did Gain lose share at Target?'
steps: Parse the question to extract brand, retailer/market, and time context
Run market share analysis at multiple granularities (category, category-manufacturer, category-manufacturer-brand) to understand the 'what'
Analyze above-the-line and below-the-line context (how is the category doing, how is the retailer doing, how are competitors doing)
Select appropriate business view (VBB, DCOM, or brand-specific) based on question context
Query Trade Panel, Decon, or other diagnostic data sources to identify root causes (price, distribution, shelf, display)
Generate HTML narrative report with findings and recommendations

decision_criteria.name: Market Selection
inputs: Question text mentioning retailer or market name
Standardized market definitions (e.g., 'Total Target', 'DCOM Target', 'In-Store Target')
criteria: If question mentions 'online' or 'digital', select DCOM market variant
If question mentions specific retailer, select that retailer's market definition
Default to 'Total [Retailer]' if no channel specified
Use 'Total USA' as comparison market for overall market context

decision_criteria.name: Product Granularity Selection
inputs: Question text (brand name, product attributes mentioned)
Brand-specific product hierarchy and attribute definitions
Available granularities: category, category-manufacturer, category-manufacturer-brand, sub-brand, form/size
criteria: If question asks about specific brand, include brand level
If question asks about sub-brand or form (e.g., 'Gain Original' vs 'Gain Fireworks'), navigate to that level if available for that brand
For Gain: do not use sub-brand attribute (not available)
For Tide: use sub-brand attribute when relevant
Always include category and category-manufacturer as baseline context

decision_criteria.name: Business View Selection
inputs: Available business views: VBB (general), DCOM (digital), brand-specific models
Data sources available: Trade Panel, Decon, HHP, others under VBB
criteria: If question implies digital/online, use DCOM business view
If question is about a specific brand with custom business model, use brand-specific view
Default to VBB for general market analysis
VBB is the generic business analysis model; DCOM and brand models are specializations underneath

decision_criteria.name: Diagnostic Data Source Selection
inputs: Available diagnostic tables: Trade Panel, Decon, HHP
Metric definitions and lineage for each source
Question focus (price, distribution, display, shelf, etc.)
criteria: Trade Panel is a data source under VBB; Decon is another
Different business views have different metrics and data structures
Select data source based on which metrics best serve the diagnostic question
Understand metric relationships and lineage to navigate from high-level metrics (e.g., ACV) to root causes (e.g., distribution points, price rotations)

decision_criteria.name: Analysis Path Routing
inputs: Available analysis paths: linear top-level, multi-granularity, above/below-the-line
criteria: Not all questions follow a linear path
Some questions require analyzing multiple product granularities in parallel
Some require above-the-line (market context) and below-the-line (brand-specific) analysis
Agent should navigate dynamically based on insights from each step, not execute all 16 AOS queries by default

escalation_paths.trigger: Question requires data or context not available in current tables (Trade Panel, Decon, AOS)
escalation_paths.handoff_target: Data engineering or central data pipelines team
artifacts_passed: Question that could not be answered

escalation_paths.trigger: Analysis reveals need for consumer insights or external market data beyond internal data sources
escalation_paths.handoff_target: Consumer insights team or Nielsen IQ team

escalation_paths.trigger: Output quality or brand voice does not meet P&G standards; requires human review before distribution to brand team
escalation_paths.handoff_target: Brand team or senior analyst for review and refinement

domain_vocabulary.AOS: All Outlets Share — market share data across all retail outlets; used in Phase 1 (brand analysis) to understand 'what happened' to a brand's share.
domain_vocabulary.Brand Analyst: A role at P&G that diagnoses why a brand's market share increased or decreased at a specific retailer or market, and produces executive narratives explaining market dynamics.
domain_vocabulary.Bootstrap Phase: [INFERRED — please confirm] Early phase of a project or initiative where foundational setup and testing occur before moving to active deployment.
domain_vocabulary.Business View: A lens or model for analyzing business performance; examples include VBB (generic business analysis), DCOM (digital commerce), and brand-specific models. Different business views have different metrics, data structures, and diagnostic capabilities.
domain_vocabulary.Category Manufacturer: A product granularity level representing the combination of product category and manufacturer; used in market share analysis to understand performance at an intermediate level of detail.
domain_vocabulary.DCOM: Digital Commerce — a business view and market variant focused on online/digital sales channels; used when questions imply digital or online context.
domain_vocabulary.Decon: A diagnostic data source (likely short for 'decomposition' or a specific P&G data table) used in Phase 2 to analyze root causes of market share changes; contains detailed transaction or point-of-sale data.
domain_vocabulary.Decision Engine: A central AI initiative within P&G's DNA (data and analytics) organization focused on building agentic systems to answer diagnostic business questions; Fabric & Home Care's use case is the lead use case for this initiative.
domain_vocabulary.Diagnostic Data Source: A table or dataset used in Phase 2 to identify root causes of market share changes; examples include Trade Panel, Decon, and HHP. Different business views reference different diagnostic sources.
domain_vocabulary.DNA: Data and Analytics — P&G's central data and analytics organization that provides capabilities and platforms to business units.
domain_vocabulary.DPSM: [INFERRED — please confirm] Likely 'Display, Price, Shelf, Merchandising' — key diagnostic dimensions used to analyze why market share changed; each dimension has associated metrics and analysis paths.
domain_vocabulary.Embedded Data Scientist: A data scientist role embedded within a specific business unit (e.g., Fabric Care) who works closely with that business unit's teams on data and analytics problems.
domain_vocabulary.Fabric & Home Care (F&HC): A P&G business unit (one of 10–12 total) focused on fabric care and home care products; serves as the lead use case for the Decision Engine initiative.
domain_vocabulary.Go-Live: The date or event when a system, project, or capability is deployed to production and made available to end users.
domain_vocabulary.Glean: A tool or system referenced in the source (likely a data discovery or search tool) used to retrieve transcripts or documentation.
domain_vocabulary.Granularity: The level of detail at which data is analyzed; examples include category, category-manufacturer, category-manufacturer-brand, sub-brand, form/size. Different granularities reveal different insights.
domain_vocabulary.HHP: [INFERRED — please confirm] Likely a data source or table name; appears to be a diagnostic data source under the VBB business view, similar to Trade Panel and Decon.
domain_vocabulary.Langraph: An agent framework (likely 'LangGraph' from LangChain) used by P&G to build agentic workflows; the preferred framework for building agents at P&G.
domain_vocabulary.Market Definition: A standardized definition of a market or market segment; examples include 'Total Target', 'DCOM Target', 'In-Store Target', 'Total USA'. Market definitions are used to filter and contextualize market share analysis.
domain_vocabulary.Market Share Analysis: Phase 1 of the brand diagnostic workflow; analyzes 'what happened' to a brand's market share by running queries at multiple granularities and market levels to establish context.
domain_vocabulary.Metric Lineage: The upstream and downstream relationships between metrics; understanding which metrics drive or are derived from other metrics. Used to navigate from high-level metrics (e.g., ACV) to root causes (e.g., distribution points).
domain_vocabulary.Minimum Viable Experiment (MVE): An early-stage project phase focused on testing technical feasibility and exploring what is possible, before advancing to a full Proof of Concept (PoC) or production.
domain_vocabulary.MCP: [INFERRED — please confirm] Likely 'Model Context Protocol' — a protocol or standard for connecting agents to external tools, APIs, and data sources (e.g., Atlan, Databricks, BigQuery).
domain_vocabulary.Ontology: A structured representation of business concepts, entities, metrics, and their relationships; used to help agents understand and navigate business knowledge and data.
domain_vocabulary.OU: Organizational Unit — a business unit or division within P&G; examples include Fabric & Home Care, Home Care, etc.
domain_vocabulary.Phase 1 (Brand Analysis): The first phase of the brand diagnostic workflow; parses the user's question, builds SQL queries at multiple granularities and markets, and produces views of market share data for downstream analysis.
domain_vocabulary.Phase 2 (Why Analysis / Root Cause Analy: The second phase of the brand diagnostic workflow; analyzes diagnostic data sources (Trade Panel, Decon) to identify root causes of market share changes (price, distribution, display, shelf).
domain_vocabulary.Phase 3 (Report Generation): The third phase of the brand diagnostic workflow; uses insights from Phases 1 and 2 to generate an HTML narrative report with findings and recommendations for the end user.
domain_vocabulary.PoC: Proof of Concept — a project phase focused on validating that a proposed solution works and delivers value before full production deployment.
domain_vocabulary.Power BI: A business intelligence and reporting tool used by P&G to create dashboards and reports; some metrics and business definitions are stored in Power BI models.
domain_vocabulary.Product Granularity: The level of detail at which products are analyzed; examples include category, category-manufacturer, category-manufacturer-brand, sub-brand, form/size. Different brands have different available granularities.
domain_vocabulary.Product Hierarchy: The structured organization of products within a brand; defines available granularities and attributes (e.g., sub-brand, form, size) for that brand.
domain_vocabulary.Question Parser: A component that extracts key information from a user's natural-language question (brand, retailer, market, time period, etc.) to inform downstream analysis steps.
domain_vocabulary.Root Cause Analysis: Phase 2 of the brand diagnostic workflow; identifies the underlying reasons (price, distribution, display, shelf, etc.) why a brand's market share changed.
domain_vocabulary.Skill: In the context of agentic systems, a skill is a reusable instruction set or expertise that defines how to accomplish a specific task (e.g., 'how to do a great market share analysis', 'how to write a great report in P&G language'). Skills are not linear workflows but playbooks that an agent can reason about and apply dynamically.
domain_vocabulary.SQL Template: A parameterized SQL query template used to generate queries at different granularities, markets, and time periods; allows the agent to dynamically construct queries based on question context.
domain_vocabulary.Sub-Brand: A product attribute representing a sub-category within a brand (e.g., 'Gain Original' vs. 'Gain Fireworks'); available for some brands (e.g., Tide) but not others (e.g., Gain).
domain_vocabulary.Tier-1 vs. Tier-2 Customer: [INFERRED — please confirm] Likely a segmentation of customers by strategic importance or size; Tier-1 being higher priority or larger customers.
domain_vocabulary.Trade Panel: A diagnostic data source used in Phase 2 to analyze root causes of market share changes; contains detailed retail transaction or point-of-sale data.
domain_vocabulary.Unity Catalog: A centralized metadata and data governance layer (likely from Databricks) used by P&G to manage and catalog centrally owned data pipelines and tables.
domain_vocabulary.VBB: A generic business view model used to analyze business performance; serves as the baseline business model with Trade Panel, Decon, and HHP as diagnostic data sources underneath it.
domain_vocabulary.Workflow: A sequence of steps or processes executed in order; in the current state, the brand diagnostic system is a workflow (deterministic, linear) rather than an agentic system (dynamic, reasoning-based).

common_edge_cases.description: Question mentions a retailer or market that maps to multiple market definitions (e.g., five different 'Target' markets with different descriptions: Total Target, DCOM, In-Store)
common_edge_cases.handling: Agent must infer from question context which variant is intended; if online/digital mentioned, select DCOM; otherwise default to Total Target. If ambiguous, may need to clarify with user or run analysis on most likely variant.

common_edge_cases.description: Brand does not have certain product attributes or granularities (e.g., Gain has no sub-brand attribute, but Tide does)
common_edge_cases.handling: Agent must know brand-specific product hierarchies and constraints; do not attempt to analyze at unavailable granularities. Use available granularities only.

common_edge_cases.description: Metric exists in multiple tables or has different definitions across business views (e.g., same metric in 20 different tables)
common_edge_cases.handling: Agent must understand metric lineage and relationships; select the metric definition appropriate to the business view and data source being used. Understand which upstream metrics drive the metric in question.

common_edge_cases.description: Data is messy, partitioned across multiple environments (central Unity Catalog, OU-specific Databricks, Power BI), with inconsistent naming conventions and business definitions
common_edge_cases.handling: Agent must navigate across central and OU-specific data; understand which tables are centrally owned vs. OU-customized; use central definitions as baseline and layer OU-specific context on top. Understand that different OUs may call the same data by different names (e.g., 'granules' vs. 'unit dose' vs. 'tile').

common_edge_cases.description: Question requires analysis at a brand-specific level with custom business context (e.g., Gain-specific price rotations, Tide-specific sub-brand analysis)
common_edge_cases.handling: Agent must have access to brand-specific business definitions and analysis methodologies; route to brand-specific business view and skills; understand that each brand has its own way of analyzing its business.

common_edge_cases.description: Generated narrative does not sound like a P&G analyst wrote it; uses vague language or incorrect terminology
common_edge_cases.handling: Implement brand voice and P&G-specific language in output generation; ensure terminology matches P&G's internal jargon and business language; review and refine output before distribution.

unwritten_rules: When building agents, start with the agent first rather than starting with skills or infrastructure, because working backwards from an agent that delivers value is more effective than building abstract components that may not be used correctly.

Default to leveraging centrally owned and maintained data pipelines and their definitions rather than having business units maintain duplicate definitions of the same tables, even if the BU adds local attributes on top.

When a metric or table exists in multiple places with the same meaning, establish a clear line between what central teams own and what the business unit customizes, rather than allowing parallel maintenance of identical definitions.

Always verify that context and descriptions are accurate before scaling an agent to production, because deterministic business questions require high confidence in outputs to avoid downstream decision-making errors.
When presenting agent outputs to end users, ensure the voice and language match how that organization's analysts would naturally speak about the business, not generic analytical language.
Start with the smallest viable set of tables and data sources rather than attempting to scan or onboard all available tables at once, to reduce scope and enable faster iteration.
Work backwards from the business question and required output to determine what data, skills, and context are actually needed, rather than building comprehensive infrastructure first.
When different business units or brands have different naming conventions or analytical approaches for the same concept, document and preserve those differences rather than forcing standardization.
Prioritize getting data AI-ready and ensuring metadata quality in the central system before attempting to build agent skills on top of it, because agent quality depends on data quality.
Run agents against messy, real data early in development to understand how they behave with actual complexity, rather than waiting for perfectly clean data.

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: Without knowing how long the full diagnostic workflow takes (Phase 1 + Phase 2 + Phase 3) or how often brand teams run these analyses, the agent cannot be designed with appropriate timeout handling, caching strategies, or user expectations around latency.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: How long does the current workflow take end-to-end from question to final HTML report? How frequently do brand teams ask these diagnostic questions—daily, weekly, ad-hoc?

flagged_unknowns.field: Escalation Trigger Thresholds for Data Gaps
flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction lists escalation to 'data engineering' when data is unavailable, but doesn't specify what constitutes 'unavailable'—is it a missing table, a missing metric, a missing time period, or something else? Without this, the agent won't know when to escalate.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: Name three specific scenarios where the agent should escalate to the data engineering team. For example: 'if a requested metric doesn't exist in any available table' or 'if the time period requested is outside the data retention window.' What are your actual escalation triggers?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction notes that output 'doesn't sound like a P&G analyst' but doesn't define what 'P&G voice' means operationally. Without specific criteria (e.g., terminology list, tone guidelines, narrative structure rules), the agent cannot be reliably evaluated or improved.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: Provide 2-3 examples of narrative reports that sound 'right' (written by P&G analysts) and 2-3 that sound 'wrong' (generic or vague). What specific phrases, structures, or terminology make the difference?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction mentions that agents need to understand 'metric lineage' to navigate from high-level metrics (e.g., ACV) to root causes, but doesn't specify the rules for when to follow lineage upstream vs. stopping at a certain level. Without this, agents may either drill too deep or not deep enough.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: If an agent is analyzing ACV (All Commodity Volume) and finds it changed, how many levels of upstream metrics should it typically traverse? Is there a stopping rule (e.g., 'stop at distribution points') or does it depend on the question?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction notes that different brands have different ways of analyzing their business (e.g., Gain vs. Tide), but doesn't specify the decision rules for which analysis path to prioritize or in what order to execute them. This is critical for agentic routing.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: For Gain specifically, if you're analyzing a market share drop at Target, in what order would you typically investigate: price rotations first, then distribution, then shelf? Or does it depend on the question? Name the standard investigation sequence for Gain.

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction mentions that some questions require 'above-the-line' (market context) and 'below-the-line' (brand-specific) analysis, but doesn't specify when to trigger each or how to decide if both are needed. Without this, the agent won't know which analysis paths to execute.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: Give an example of a question that would require only above-the-line analysis, one that requires only below-the-line, and one that requires both. What signals in the question determine which path to take?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction notes that the agent should analyze 'multiple product granularities in parallel' but doesn't specify when to stop analyzing or how to know when enough granularities have been examined. Without stopping criteria, the agent could run indefinitely.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: If you're analyzing Gain at Target and you've already looked at category, category-manufacturer, and category-manufacturer-brand levels, when would you stop? Would you ever drill down to form/size, or is brand the lowest level you'd go?

flagged_unknowns.field: Data Source Selection Criteria for Diagnostic Analysis
flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction lists Trade Panel, Decon, and HHP as diagnostic sources but doesn't specify the decision rules for which source to query based on the question or the metric being diagnosed. Without this, the agent won't know which table to use.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: If the question is 'Why did Gain's distribution points drop at Target?', which diagnostic data source would you query first—Trade Panel, Decon, or HHP? What question patterns map to each source?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction notes that 'evaluation is another thing we're working through' and that outputs must be 'accurate enough to feed to end users,' but doesn't define what 'accurate' means or what threshold triggers a report being held back from distribution.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: How would you measure whether a diagnostic report is accurate? For example, do you compare the agent's findings against a human analyst's findings? What accuracy threshold (e.g., 90% agreement) would you require before releasing a report to the brand team?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction lists escalation to 'consumer insights team' when analysis reveals need for external data, but doesn't specify what types of findings trigger this escalation or what questions the consumer insights team would answer.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: Give an example of a market share diagnostic finding that would require escalation to the consumer insights team. What specific consumer behavior questions would they be asked to answer?

flagged_unknowns.field: Central vs. OU-Specific Data Ownership Boundaries
flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction notes tension between central data pipelines and OU-specific customizations, but doesn't specify the governance rule for when Fabric Care should maintain its own version of a metric vs. using the central definition. Without this, the agent won't know which definition to trust.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: If Trade Panel is owned centrally but Fabric Care has added OU-specific columns or renamed fields, which version should the agent use by default? How should the agent handle conflicts between central and OU definitions?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction notes that questions can be ambiguous (e.g., 'Target' could map to five different market definitions), but doesn't specify the fallback logic when ambiguity can't be resolved. Should the agent ask for clarification, make an assumption, or run multiple analyses?
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: If a brand team asks 'Why did Gain lose share at Target?' without specifying online or in-store, what should the agent do: ask for clarification, default to Total Target, or run both analyses and present both?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction mentions 'HTML narrative report with findings and recommendations' but doesn't specify the required sections, order, or content structure. Without this, the agent won't know how to organize the output.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: What are the required sections in a diagnostic report? For example: Executive Summary, Market Context, Brand Performance, Root Cause Analysis, Recommendations. In what order should they appear?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction mentions 'recommendations' as part of the output but doesn't specify what types of recommendations are in scope (pricing, distribution, promotion, etc.) or who validates them before they reach the brand team.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: Should the agent generate actionable recommendations (e.g., 'increase shelf space by 10%') or only diagnostic findings (e.g., 'shelf space declined 15%')? Who reviews recommendations before they're included in the report?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction mentions three personas (analysts, executives, brand teams) but doesn't specify what output variations each persona should receive. Without this, the agent won't know how to tailor reports by audience.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: If an executive asks the same question as an analyst, what should be different in the report? Should the executive get a one-page summary while the analyst gets a 10-page deep dive? What are the specific differences?

flagged_unknowns.field: Feedback Loop and Model Retraining Triggers
flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction mentions 'lose its feedback loop' as a concern but doesn't specify how feedback is collected, what triggers model retraining, or how often the agent's context should be refreshed.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: How will you collect feedback on whether the agent's diagnostic findings were correct? When a brand team validates or disputes the agent's findings, how does that feedback get back into the system to improve future analyses?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction doesn't specify how fresh the data needs to be for diagnostic analysis. If a brand team asks 'Why did Gain lose share at Target?' on Monday, should the analysis include data through Friday or only through the previous week?
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: What is the acceptable data latency for market share diagnostics? For example, should the agent analyze data through yesterday, last week, or last month? Does this vary by question type?

flagged_unknowns.field: Handling of Conflicting Metrics Across Tables
flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction notes that 'the same metric exists in multiple tables with different definitions' but doesn't specify how the agent should handle conflicts. Should it flag the conflict, use a priority order, or ask for clarification?
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: If ACV is defined differently in Trade Panel vs. Decon, which definition should the agent use? Should it flag the discrepancy in the report, or silently use one definition?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction mentions that different OUs call the same data by different names (e.g., 'granules' vs. 'unit dose' vs. 'tile') but doesn't specify how the agent should handle these mappings or where the mapping is stored.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: For Fabric Care, what are the three most common naming convention differences between central definitions and Fabric Care's local terminology? Where should these mappings be stored so the agent can use them?

flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction emphasizes that 'deterministic business questions require high confidence in outputs' but doesn't specify what confidence threshold is acceptable (e.g., 95%, 99%) or what happens if the agent can't reach that threshold.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: What is the minimum confidence level you'd require before the agent includes a finding in a report? If the agent is only 70% confident in a root cause, should it include it, flag it as uncertain, or exclude it?

flagged_unknowns.field: Skill Composition and Reusability Across Use Cases
flagged_unknowns.why_it_matters: The extraction mentions converting 'instructions in langraph to skills' but doesn't specify which instructions should become skills, how granular they should be, or which skills should be reusable across other F&HC use cases.
flagged_unknowns.probe_suggestion: Of the current workflow steps (question parsing, market share analysis, diagnostic analysis, report generation), which should become standalone skills that could be reused by other agents? Which are specific to this use case?

target_use_case: Brand analyst agent that answers diagnostic market-share questions like 'Why did Gain lose share at Target?' end-to-end. The analyst queries AOS market share data for the 'what happened' phase and Trade Panel / Decon data for the 'why it happened' (root cause) phase. Output is an executive narrative for the F&HC brand team.
